senses.definition: Likely an incomplete or misspelled form of 'approachable', not used as a standard word in contemporary English.
examples: The string 'approachabl' looks like a typing error for 'approachable'.
You should write 'approachable', not 'approachabl', in formal texts.

senses.definition: Friendly and easy to talk to.
examples: Our teacher is very approachable, so students feel comfortable asking questions.
The manager seems strict but is actually quite approachable.

senses.definition: Easy to understand or enjoy, especially about ideas, books, or art.
examples: The book explains complex theories in an approachable way.
The museum aims to make modern art more approachable to the public.

senses.definition: The quality of being friendly and easy to talk to.
examples: Her approachableness makes new employees feel welcome.
Approachableness is an important trait in good leaders.

senses.definition: Came nearer in distance or time.
examples: As we approached the city, the traffic became heavier.
Winter approached and the days grew shorter.

senses.definition: Came to speak to someone, especially to ask or offer something.
examples: She approached her boss about taking a few days off.
Several companies approached him with job offers.

senses.definition: Began to deal with or think about a problem or task in a particular way.
examples: He approached the problem from a different angle.
The project was approached in several stages.

senses.definition: A person or thing that approaches, especially someone who comes toward another person.
examples: He waited to see whether the approacher would speak to him.
The dog barked at every approacher near the gate.

senses.definition: People or things that approach, especially people who come toward someone.
examples: The guards watched the road for any approachers.
Approachers were checked before entering the building.

senses.definition: Comes nearer in distance or time.
examples: As night approaches, the temperature drops.
The train approaches the station at high speed.

senses.definition: Comes to speak to someone, especially to ask or offer something.
examples: If a stranger approaches you, be careful.
He usually approaches his manager with solutions, not just problems.

senses.definition: Deals with a problem or task in a particular way.
examples: She approaches each project with great enthusiasm.
The team approaches the issue from a practical viewpoint.

senses.definition: Ways of doing or thinking about something.
examples: Different cultures have different approaches to education.
We discussed several possible approaches to the problem.

senses.definition: Coming nearer in distance or time.
examples: A storm is approaching from the west.
Her birthday is approaching, and she is planning a party.

senses.definition: Soon to happen or coming closer in time.
examples: They are nervous about the approaching exams.
With the approaching deadline, everyone is working late.

senses.definition: Having no way to be reached or no way of approaching, or impossible to approach in character.
examples: The castle stood on a steep, approachless rock.
To the young intern, the director seemed cold and approachless.

senses.definition: The act of coming nearer to something or someone, especially in a formal or literary style.
examples: The approachment of winter was marked by strong winds.
Their approachment to the city was delayed by heavy snow.

senses.definition: To officially approve or accept something.
examples: The committee must approbate the new policy before it is implemented.
The contract was finally approbated by the court.

senses.definition: Officially approved or accepted something.
examples: The board approbated the merger after months of discussion.
The judge approbated the settlement agreement.

senses.definition: Officially approves or accepts something.
examples: The council approbates the budget at the end of each year.
The court approbates only those agreements that meet legal standards.

senses.definition: Officially approving or accepting something.
examples: The committee is currently approbating the new guidelines.
By approbating the plan, they signaled their full support.

senses.definition: Official approval or praise.
examples: Her proposal received the full approbation of the board.
The artist won public approbation for his latest work.

senses.definition: Acts or expressions of official approval or praise.
examples: Their efforts earned them many approbations from community leaders.
The policy changes received approbations from both sides of the parliament.

senses.definition: Showing approval or praise.
examples: The critics wrote highly approbative reviews of the performance.
He greeted her with an approbative nod.

senses.definition: The tendency to approve of or praise things, or a desire to receive approval.
examples: His strong approbativeness made him eager for public recognition.
Writers often balance honesty with approbativeness toward their readers.

senses.definition: A person or body that gives official approval.
examples: The approbator must sign the document before it becomes valid.
The agency acts as an approbator of new drugs.

senses.definition: Expressing approval or agreement.
examples: Her speech was followed by approbatory applause.
He gave her an approbatory comment on her research.

senses.definition: To encourage or stimulate someone to think of or do something, especially in an old or rare use.
examples: The teacher used a story to apprompt their imagination.
Such questions apprompt deeper reflection.

senses.definition: Approval or proof, especially in old use.
examples: He sought the approof of his peers before acting.
Their success was an approof of the new method.

senses.definition: Acts or signs of approval or proof, in old use.
examples: His many approofs convinced them of his loyalty.
They took the repeated successes as approofs of their theory.

senses.definition: To come near to something or someone, especially in an old or very formal style.
examples: As they appropinquated the town, the road grew narrow.
The ship slowly appropinquated the harbor at dawn.

senses.definition: Came near to something or someone, especially in an old or very formal style.
examples: They appropinquated the village just before sunset.
As the army appropinquated the city, the people prepared to flee.

senses.definition: Comes near to something or someone, especially in an old or very formal style.
examples: The road appropinquates the river at this point.
As night appropinquates, the air turns colder.

senses.definition: Coming near to something or someone, especially in an old or very formal style.
examples: Appropinquating the coast, they could see the lights of the harbor.
The storm, appropinquating fast, forced them to seek shelter.

senses.definition: The act of coming near to something or someone, in an old or very formal style.
examples: The sailors watched the gradual appropinquation of land.
With the appropinquation of night, they made camp.

senses.definition: To come near or approach, especially in an old or rare use.
examples: They resolved to appropinque the castle under cover of darkness.
As we appropinque the summit, the path becomes steeper.

senses.definition: Came near or approached, in an old or rare use.
examples: They appropinqued the fortress at dawn.
As we appropinqued the village, we heard music.

senses.definition: Comes near or approaches, in an old or rare use.
examples: The path appropinques the river at this point.
As the storm appropinques, the sky grows dark.

senses.definition: Coming near or approaching, in an old or rare use.
examples: Appropinquing the city, they saw the lights in the distance.
The appropinquing danger made them hurry.

senses.definition: Instances of physical or relationship nearness, especially in old or formal style.
examples: Urban life creates many appropinquities between strangers.
The appropinquities of their offices led to frequent cooperation.

senses.definition: Nearness in place or relationship.
examples: The appropinquity of their desks helped them become friends.
Physical appropinquity often leads to emotional closeness.

senses.definition: A non-standard or obsolete form related to 'appropriate', not used in contemporary standard English.
examples: The form 'appropre' appears only in very old texts.
Modern English uses 'appropriate' instead of 'appropre'.

senses.definition: Able to be taken for a particular use, purpose, or owner.
examples: Only certain funds are legally appropriable for this project.
The data is not publicly appropriable without permission.

senses.definition: Ways in which language or behavior is suitable for a particular situation, especially in teaching.
examples: Teachers discuss different appropriacies of language in formal and informal emails.
The book focuses on the appropriacies of tone in academic writing.

senses.definition: The quality of being suitable or correct for a particular situation, especially in language use.
examples: Learners must consider the appropriacy of informal phrases in exams.
We discussed the appropriacy of using first names in business emails.

senses.definition: The act of making something suitable or fitting, in an old or rare use.
examples: The appropriament of his speech to the audience was impressive.
Such careful appropriament of style is rare today.

senses.definition: Right or suitable for a particular time, situation, or person.
examples: Jeans are not appropriate for a formal interview.
Please use language that is appropriate for young children.

senses.definition: To take or use something, often without permission or without the right to do so.
examples: The company was accused of appropriating indigenous designs.
He was fired for appropriating some of the firm’s funds.

senses.definition: To officially set money, time, or resources aside for a particular purpose.
examples: The government appropriated extra funds for education.
The council has appropriated land for a new park.

senses.definition: Took or used something, often without permission or without the right to do so.
examples: Several traditional symbols were appropriated for commercial use.
They claimed that the artist had appropriated their work.

senses.definition: Officially set money, time, or resources aside for a particular purpose.
examples: The legislature appropriated funds for the new highway.
A large amount of money was appropriated to support research.

senses.definition: In a way that is right or suitable for a particular time, situation, or person.
examples: She dressed appropriately for the job interview.
The question was politely and appropriately answered.

senses.definition: The quality of being right or suitable for a particular situation or purpose.
examples: We discussed the appropriateness of his remarks.
Teachers should consider the appropriateness of materials for younger learners.

senses.definition: Different ways in which something can be suitable for particular situations.
examples: The writer explores the appropriatenesses of various tones in business letters.
They debated the appropriatenesses of possible responses.

senses.definition: Takes or uses something, often without permission or without the right to do so.
examples: The film appropriates images from many different cultures.
He regularly appropriates ideas from his colleagues.

senses.definition: Officially sets money, time, or resources aside for a particular purpose.
examples: The government appropriates billions of dollars for health care each year.
The committee appropriates funds to local projects.

senses.definition: Taking or using something, often without permission or without the right to do so.
examples: The company was criticized for appropriating traditional patterns.
Artists must be careful when appropriating images from other cultures.

senses.definition: Officially setting money, time, or resources aside for a particular purpose.
examples: The council is appropriating funds for the new library.
By appropriating more money to education, they hope to improve standards.

senses.definition: The act of taking something, especially money or ideas, for your own use, often without permission.
examples: There was a heated debate about the cultural appropriation of traditional music.
He was accused of appropriation of company funds.

senses.definition: An amount of money that a government or organization officially sets aside for a particular purpose.
examples: The education appropriation was increased this year.
Parliament approved the appropriations for defense spending.

senses.definition: Amounts of money that a government or organization officially sets aside for particular purposes.
examples: The committee discussed appropriations for health and education.
These programs depend on annual appropriations from Congress.

senses.definition: Acts of taking something, especially money or ideas, for your own use, often without permission.
examples: The artist’s works are playful appropriations of advertising images.
Unauthorized appropriations of data can damage public trust.

senses.definition: Related to taking something for one’s own use, or to assigning money or resources for a purpose.
examples: Some critics see modern art as highly appropriative.
The appropriative powers of the legislature are limited by law.

senses.definition: The quality of being suitable or fitting for a particular situation, or of tending to take things for one’s own use, depending on context.
examples: They praised the appropriativness of his language in the report.
Some scholars discuss the appropriativness of colonial policies.
